raw_response:
Orders (S1901):
- F LON -> ENG
- F EDI -> NTH
- A LVP -> YOR

Rationale (short): Hold the two sea lanes that matter to our security — English Channel and North Sea — while putting an army into Yorkshire as a flexible continental staging point. This posture denies France an easy Channel foothold, projects naval pressure toward Germany’s northern flank, and keeps our options open for autumn diplomacy.

Suggested short diplomatic notes (understated, firm, maritime tone)

To France (Bre/Par/Mar):
"I shall keep a fleet in the Channel and one in the North Sea this spring. If you refrain from moving Brest into the Channel, we can avoid costly clashes and coordinate on mutual interests (Spain/Italy or colonial waters). A quiet Channel benefits us both."

To Germany (Kie/Ber/ Mun):
"My fleets will be in the North Sea and Channel; I have no appetite for an early continental naval war. Please avoid moves into Skagerrak/Denmark this spring — doing so risks a firm naval reply. If you prefer, we can agree to respect each other’s northern coasts and look at cooperation elsewhere."

To Russia (Sev/Mos/Stp/War):
"I am occupied with sea lanes to my west and north; I have no immediate plans against you in Scandinavia if you avoid contesting Norway. Neutrality there keeps supply lines safe for both of us."

Tactical forecast to shape negotiations:
- With F EDI -> NTH + F LON -> ENG, my fleets can interdict German moves into Skagerrak/Denmark and contest any Brest->ENG attempt. A German move KIE->SKA or DEN risks a swift English fleet response to hold the North Sea. If France moves Brest->ENG, expect a direct fleet clash; if Germany moves into Scandinavia, I will consider moving from NTH to SKA/NDL to contest.

If you want, I can draft any of those messages in a more personal tone or send additional notes to Austria, Italy or Turkey to shore up neutrality elsewhere.
